content:
I'm currently a Google AI Resident in the Brain team in Montreal. My mentor is Laurent Dinh and we are working on generative models for unspervised object discovery.
In the past, I complited my master's at University of Amsterdam, where I worked with Rianne van den Berg (AMLAB) on priors for VAEs and semi-supervised learning, and wrote my thesis during an internship at Mila, where I worked with Devon Hjelm on fundamental principles in Representation Learning for Zero Shot Learning.
In my free time I practice yoga, acroyoga and read a lot.
summary:
I'm currently a Google AI Resident in the Brain team in Montreal. My mentor is Laurent Dinh and we are working on generative models for unspervised object discovery.
In the past, I complited my master's at University of Amsterdam, where I worked with Rianne van den Berg (AMLAB) on priors for VAEs and semi-supervised learning, and wrote my thesis during an internship at Mila, where I worked with Devon Hjelm on fundamental principles in Representation Learning for Zero Shot Learning.
title: Linda Petrini

summary: In this work we study locality and compositionality in the context of learning representations for Zero Shot Learning (ZSL).  In order to well-isolate the importance of these properties in learned representations, we impose the additional constraint that, differently from most recent work in ZSL, no pre-training on different datasets (e.g. ImageNet) is performed. The results of our experiment show how locality, in terms of small parts of the input, and compositionality, i.e. how well can the learned representations be expressed as a function of a smaller vocabulary, are both deeply related to generalization and motivate the focus on more local-aware models in future research directions for representation learning.
title: Locality and Compositionality in Zero-Shot Learning

summary: Interpretability is a major concern when applying deep convolutional neural networks to image data. In this work we introduce a tool based on recent advances in Mutual Information estimation that makes it possible to investigate which parts of an image are important for the learned representations when trying to distinguish it from another. This pairwise approach is evaluated on a set of supervised and unsupervised representation learning models and leads to qualitative insights into the specificities of each representation. In particular, we find that very different supervised learning algorithms are able to recover semantic structure and that reconstruction-based models such as VAEs are more sensitive to pixel similarity rather than semantic proximity, which would most likely be detrimental to generalization.
title: Mutual Information heatmaps as a tool for interpretability
